Table of contents

  • 1 Read in and process the data
    • 1.1 Set libraries and directories
  • 2 This analysis uses 2023 ACS data from IPUMS USA, University of Minnesota, www.ipums.org

    • 2.2 Create a previous state of residence variable for counting 2022 population
  • 3 State Migration Rates
    • 3.1 Calculate migration rates by origin state
    • 3.2 Read in shapefile and join the data to the shapefile
    • 3.3 Map state migration rates
  • 4 State migration rates by education
    • 4.1 Now lets compute migration rates in states by education
    • 4.2 Calculate some location quotients
    • 4.3 Join the migration by state by education data to the shapefile
    • 4.4 Map of state rates by education
    • 4.5 Location quotients of state migration rates by education relative to overall state migration rates
    • 4.6 Location quotients of state migration rates by education relative to national migration rates by education

    • 4.9 Calculate and make a dataframe for our age cohorts
    • 4.10 Perform a right join to keep everything in the migrate_age_cohorts and plot our grid again of our 4 selected age cohorts
    • 4.11 Calculate location quotient compared to total rates by State and graph that according to each age group
    • 4.12 Calculate national rate of migration for age cohorts and compare that by state. I used the none shapefile because there was weird issues here with the geometry messing with the variable being found.
    • 4.13 Now we will go ahead and merge this issue to our shapefile, compute the location quotient of national migration rates of the age cohorts compared to the state age cohort migration rate

Migration Rates by State by Education, Ethnicity, and Age

Source: American Community Survey 2023

Author

Mark Ellis

Published

Feb 12, 2026

1 Read in and process the data

1.1 Set libraries and directories

2 This analysis uses 2023 ACS data from IPUMS USA, University of Minnesota, www.ipums.org

Code 
library(ipumsr)
library(tidyverse)
library(gt)
library(scales)
library(sf) 

# wkdir is the root of your project directory
wkdir <- here::here()

# set directories for where you store data and save plots relative to project root
dataDir <- file.path(wkdir, "data")  #read data file dir
plotsDir <- file.path(wkdir, "plots")  #save plot files dir

options(scipen=99, digits=5)

# create plots directory if it does not exist
ifelse(!dir.exists(file.path(wkdir, "plots")), dir.create(file.path(wkdir, "plots")), FALSE)


options(scipen=99, digits = 5)

2.1 Read the data

Use the same extract download as for the first assignment

This analysis uses 2023 ACS data from IPUMS USA, University of Minnesota, www.ipums.org.

Code 
if (!require("ipumsr")) stop("Reading IPUMS data into R requires the ipumsr package. It can be installed using the following command: install.packages('ipumsr')")

dataSet <- "usa_00001.xml"  # name of YOUR 2023 xml file

ddi <- read_ipums_ddi(file.path(dataDir,dataSet)) # ACS 2023
data <- read_ipums_micro(ddi)
data <- data %>% 
  filter(YEAR != 2000)

# extract variable names the traditional way (useful reference)
varname <- as.data.frame(colnames(data))

# create webpage of variable names, labels and values, save it as dataSet.html in the data directory
# useful for reference
ipums_view(ddi, launch = FALSE, out_file=file.path(wkdir, "data", paste0(dataSet,".html" )))

# add a state name variable to data 
# first, create a dataframe with statefips codes (as val) and statenames (as lbl) from variable STATEIP
STATES <- ipums_val_labels(ddi, STATEFIP) 

# then, join STATES to data (using: STATEFIP=val) and rename new variable lbl as STATENAME
data <- data %>% 
  left_join(select(STATES, STATEFIP=val, STATENAME=lbl))

2.2 Create a previous state of residence variable for counting 2022 population

  • MIGPLAC1 is state of residence 1 year ago. It has a code of 0 for those whose current state is the same as the state 1 year ago.

  • Assign STATEFIP to MIGPLAC1 for those whose state of residence in the sample year is the same as 1 year prior (i.e. those who didn’t move across state lines)

    • If we don’t do this all those who didn’t migrate will retain a MIGPLAC1 code of 0 and will not be counted as part of the population of the state lived in the prior year.

  • Create a new variable called PREV_STATENAME from the labels for MIGPLAC1; these are the names of the states people lived in 1 year prior

Code 
# assign current state of residence to previous state for those who didn't move
# (i.e. MIGPLAC1=0)
data <- data %>% 
#  filter(AGE>=1) %>% 
  mutate(MIGPLAC1=case_when(
    AGE>=1 & MIGPLAC1 == 0 ~ STATEFIP,
    TRUE ~ as.integer(MIGPLAC1)))


# make a new variable called PREV_STATENAME from MIGPLAC1 labels
MIGPLAC1 <- ipums_val_labels(ddi, MIGPLAC1)  

# add MIGPLAC1 labels to our data extract as new variable PREV_STATENAME
data$PREV_STATENAME <- as_factor(data$MIGPLAC1)

3 State Migration Rates

3.1 Calculate migration rates by origin state

Using PREV_STATENAME as our grouping variable, we can correctly calculate migration rates based on where people lived before they migrated i.e. 1 year prior.

Code 
migrate_state_origin <- data %>% 
  filter(AGE>=1 & MIGPLAC1<=56) %>% # those >= 1 and those who lived in the US 1 year prior
  group_by(PREV_STATENAME) %>% 
  summarise(pop_origin=sum(PERWT),
         interstate_movers_origin=sum(PERWT[MIGRATE1==3])) %>%
  mutate(interstate_mig_rate_origin=interstate_movers_origin/pop_origin)

gt(head(migrate_state_origin))
State (FIPS code) pop_origin interstate_movers_origin interstate_mig_rate_origin
Alabama 5025459 101870 0.020271
Alaska 718714 36206 0.050376
Arizona 7247081 193757 0.026736
Arkansas 3014253 65211 0.021634
California 38496762 682598 0.017731
Colorado 5757032 209052 0.036312

3.2 Read in shapefile and join the data to the shapefile

Read in the same shapefile I shared with you in Week 4. It should be in a Shapefiles directory under the directory where you save your codefile.

Join migrate_state_origin to the shapefile shapes_shp

Code 
# Read shapefile
states_shp <- st_read(file.path(wkdir, "Shapefiles", "states_moveAKHI.shp"),"states_moveAKHI", quiet = TRUE)

# join the migration rate by origin data to the shapefile 
states_shp <- states_shp %>% 
  left_join(migrate_state_origin, by = c("STATE_NAME" = "PREV_STATENAME"))

3.3 Map state migration rates

This map shows the highest migration rates are in the intermountain west states and Alaska and North Dakota. There are also high rates Virginia and Vermont. The lowest rates are in California, Texas, and parts of the upper midwest.

Code 
p2 <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = states_shp, aes(fill=interstate_mig_rate_origin), size=0.1) +
  coord_sf(crs = "epsg:5070") +
  geom_segment(aes(x = -2250000, y = 1050000, xend = -1500000, yend = 900000), size=0.2) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = -1500000, y = 900000, xend = -600000, yend = 400000), size=0.2) +
  scale_fill_distiller(name=' ', palette='BuPu',direction=1,
                      breaks=c(.0, .01, .02, .03, .04, .05, .06, .07),
                      labels=label_percent(accuracy = 1),
                      limits=c(0,.07)) +
  labs(title = "Outmigration rate by state, 2022-2023") +
  theme(panel.grid.major = element_blank(), 
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        panel.background = element_blank(), 
        axis.line = element_blank(), 
        axis.text.x=element_blank(),
        axis.text.y=element_blank(),
        axis.ticks=element_blank(),
        axis.title=element_blank(),
        legend.key.size =  unit(0.5, "in"),
        legend.text = element_text(size=12),
        legend.title = element_text(size=10),
        plot.title = element_text(size=14,hjust = 0.5),
        plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5),        
        plot.margin=unit(c(1, 1, 1, 1), "points"),
        strip.background = element_rect(fill='white'),
        strip.text.x = element_text(size = 10)) 
Warning: Using `size` aesthetic for lines was deprecated in ggplot2 3.4.0.
ℹ Please use `linewidth` instead.
Code 
p2

4 State migration rates by education

4.1 Now lets compute migration rates in states by education

We calculate four rates using a series of differing group_bys, summarises and mutates, ungrouping when necessary to apply a new group_by

The four rates we calculate are:

  • interstate_mig_rate_state_edlevel: state migration rates by education
  • interstate_mig_rate_all: the national migration rate (overall, not by education)
  • interstate_mig_rate_state: state migration rates (overall, not by education)
  • interstate_mig_rate_edlevel: national migration rates by education

We will map the first of these only: interstate_mig_rate_state_edlevel. The other rates are useful as denominators in location quotients - see later section.

Code 
# compute state migration rates: all, by state,  by state and education level
migrate_educ_states <- data %>% 
  filter(AGE>=25 & MIGPLAC1 <=56) %>% # those >= 1 and those who lived in the US 1 year prior
  mutate(Education=case_when(
    EDUCD <= 64 ~ "HS or Less",
    EDUCD >=65 & EDUCD <=100 ~ "Some College",
    EDUCD >= 101 & EDUCD <= 113 ~ "BA degree",
    EDUCD >= 114 ~ "Advanced Degree")) %>% 
  group_by(PREV_STATENAME, Education) %>% 
  summarise(pop_state_edlevel=sum(PERWT),
         interstate_movers_state_edlevel=sum(PERWT[MIGRATE1==3])) %>%
  mutate(interstate_mig_rate_state_edlevel=interstate_movers_state_edlevel/pop_state_edlevel) %>% 
  ungroup() %>% 
  mutate(interstate_mig_rate_all=sum(interstate_movers_state_edlevel)/sum(pop_state_edlevel)) %>% 
  group_by(PREV_STATENAME) %>% 
  mutate(interstate_mig_rate_state=sum(interstate_movers_state_edlevel)/sum(pop_state_edlevel)) %>% 
  group_by(Education) %>% 
  mutate(interstate_mig_rate_edlevel=sum(interstate_movers_state_edlevel)/sum(pop_state_edlevel))
`summarise()` has grouped output by 'PREV_STATENAME'. You can override using
the `.groups` argument.

4.2 Calculate some location quotients

For further analysis lets compute some LQs of these migration rates. These are the rates of migration by education in each state divided by a reference rate.

Values of 1 indicate that the state rate of migration by education equals the reference rate. Values below 1 indicate the state rate of migration by education is below the reference rate. Values above one means its above the reference rate.

We will calculate two location quotients:

  • state migration rates by education level / overall state migration rate

  • state migration rates by education / national migration rates by education

These are added to the migrate_educ_states dataframe we created in the previous code chunk

Code 
migrate_educ_states <-migrate_educ_states %>% 
  ungroup() %>% 
  mutate(lq_interstate_mig_rate_state=interstate_mig_rate_state_edlevel/interstate_mig_rate_state) %>% 
  mutate(lq_interstate_mig_rate_edlevel=interstate_mig_rate_state_edlevel/interstate_mig_rate_edlevel)

4.3 Join the migration by state by education data to the shapefile

Note the right_join. See comments in code.

Code 
# join our rates and lqs to the shapefile (we must use a right join here because we
# want to keep all rows in migrate_educ_states)
states_shp2 <- states_shp %>% 
  right_join(migrate_educ_states, by = c("STATE_NAME" = "PREV_STATENAME"))

4.4 Map of state rates by education

Here we use facet_wrap to map state rates of migration by education on one image with four panels.

Compare the ggplot code here with the ggplot code used to produce the first map above to see how the code has been tweaked to make this work.

There is a lot of information in these four panels.

Generally speaking, you can see most states have higher outmigration rates (darker shades of blue and purples) the higher the level of education. There are some standout states for each education level. The upper midwest and intermountain west have high rates of outmigration by those with advanced degrees. Those with BAs have the highest outmigration rates from the intermountain west and Mississippi and Virginia. At the other end of the education scale, those with a high school education or less have the highest outmigration rates from Montana and N Dakota; Texas, California and parts of the midwest and deep south have very low rates for this group.

Code 
# join our rates and lqs to the shapefile (we use a right join here because we
# want to keep all rows in migrate_educ_states)
states_shp2 <- states_shp %>% 
  right_join(migrate_educ_states, by = c("STATE_NAME" = "PREV_STATENAME"))

ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = states_shp2, aes(fill=interstate_mig_rate_state_edlevel), size=0.1) +
  coord_sf(crs = "epsg:5070") +
  scale_fill_distiller(name=' ', palette='BuPu',direction=1,
                      breaks=c(.0, .01, .02, .03, .04, .05, .06, .07, .08),
                      labels=label_percent(accuracy = 1),
                      limits=c(0,.08)) +
  labs(title = "Migration rates, 2022-2023") +
  theme(panel.grid.major = element_blank(), 
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        panel.background = element_blank(), 
        axis.line = element_blank(), 
        axis.text.x=element_blank(),
        axis.text.y=element_blank(),
        axis.ticks=element_blank(),
        axis.title=element_blank(),
        legend.key.size =  unit(0.25, "in"),
        legend.text = element_text(size=8),
        legend.title = element_text(size=6),
        plot.title = element_text(size=14,hjust = 0.5),
        plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5),        
        plot.margin=unit(c(1, 1, 1, 1), "points"),
        strip.background = element_rect(fill='white'),
        strip.text.x = element_text(size = 10)) +
  facet_wrap(~Education)

4.5 Location quotients of state migration rates by education relative to overall state migration rates

This maps lq_interstate_mig_rate_state: calculated as State migration rate by education / Overall state migration rate.

It show whether an education group’s migration rate in a state departs from the overall state migration rate and how how much.

This map of location quotients allows us to see how much each education group’s migration rate differs from the aggregate state migration rate. You can see the purples for those with a BA or advanced degree, meaning their group rates are above the state rate, unsurprisingly. This is the case in a cluster of states un the middle and midwest of the country. The shades of purple are lighter in Washington, California and other states, meaning those with BAs or advanced degrees are not apt to leave these states at higher rates than other educational groups. This is perhaps a sign that these state’s economies can absorb highly educated labor more readily.

Code 
ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = states_shp2, aes(fill=lq_interstate_mig_rate_state), size=0.1) +
  coord_sf(crs = "epsg:5070") +
  scale_fill_gradient2(name=' ', low="red", mid="white", high="blue", 
                       midpoint=1, limits=c(0,4)
  ) +
  labs(title = "Migration rates relative to overall state migration rate, 2022-2023",
       subtitle = "State migration rate by education / Overall state migration rate") +
  theme(panel.grid.major = element_blank(), 
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        panel.background = element_blank(), 
        axis.line = element_blank(), 
        axis.text.x=element_blank(),
        axis.text.y=element_blank(),
        axis.ticks=element_blank(),
        axis.title=element_blank(),
        legend.key.size =  unit(0.25, "in"),
        legend.text = element_text(size=8),
        legend.title = element_text(size=6),
        plot.title = element_text(size=14,hjust = 0.5),
        plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5),        
        plot.margin=unit(c(1, 1, 1, 1), "points"),
        strip.background = element_rect(fill='white'),
        strip.text.x = element_text(size = 10)) +
  facet_wrap(~Education)

4.6 Location quotients of state migration rates by education relative to national migration rates by education

This maps lq_interstate_mig_rate_educ: calculated as State migration rate by education / National migration rate by education

This map’s location quotients show whether the state migration rates for an educational group are higher or lower than those for the educational group nationally. The intermountain west and plains states are shades of purple on all maps, suggesting all educational groups have higher rates of outmigration from these states than their national equivalents. Alaska also really stands out in this regard. California, Texas, and the upper midwest have lower outmigration rates at all education levels than their national equivalents.

Code 
ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = states_shp2, aes(fill=lq_interstate_mig_rate_edlevel), size=0.1) +
  coord_sf(crs = "epsg:5070") +
  scale_fill_gradient2(name='',
                       low="red", mid="white", high="blue", 
                       midpoint=1, limits=c(0,4)
  ) +
  labs(title = "Migration rates relative to national migration rate by education, 2022-2023",
       subtitle = "State migration rate by education / National migration rate by education") +
  theme(panel.grid.major = element_blank(), 
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        panel.background = element_blank(), 
        axis.line = element_blank(), 
        axis.text.x=element_blank(),
        axis.text.y=element_blank(),
        axis.ticks=element_blank(),
        axis.title=element_blank(),
        legend.key.size =  unit(0.25, "in"),
        legend.text = element_text(size=8),
        legend.title = element_text(size=6),
        plot.title = element_text(size=14,hjust = 0.5),
        plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5),        
        plot.margin=unit(c(1, 1, 1, 1), "points"),
        strip.background = element_rect(fill='white'),
        strip.text.x = element_text(size = 10)) +
  facet_wrap(~Education)

4.7 We make a new data frame that separates data by ethnicity

Code 
migrate_race <- data %>% 
  filter(AGE>=1) %>% 
  mutate(RACE_ETHNIC=case_when(
    HISPAN==0 & RACE==1 ~ "White",
    HISPAN==0 & RACE==2 ~ "Black",
    HISPAN==0 & RACE==3 ~ "American Indian",
    HISPAN==0 & (RACE>=4 & RACE <=6) ~ "Asian",
    HISPAN==0 & RACE==7 ~ "Other",
    HISPAN==0 & RACE>=8 ~ "Mixed",
    HISPAN>0 ~ "Latino")) %>% 
  group_by(RACE_ETHNIC, PREV_STATENAME) %>% 
  summarise(pop=sum(PERWT),
         interstate_movers=sum(PERWT[MIGRATE1==3])) %>%
  mutate(interstate_mig_rate_state_race=interstate_movers/pop)
`summarise()` has grouped output by 'RACE_ETHNIC'. You can override using the
`.groups` argument.
Code 
gt(head(migrate_race)) %>% 
  opt_table_font(size = 40) %>% 
  cols_align(
    align = "center",
  )
State (FIPS code) pop interstate_movers interstate_mig_rate_state_race
American Indian
Alabama 12990 737 0.0567360
Alaska 100361 1026 0.0102231
Arizona 226328 2157 0.0095304
Arkansas 11871 386 0.0325162
California 103313 3488 0.0337615
Colorado 19802 967 0.0488335

4.8 Now we use a right join again to keep everything from the race migration data and filter out some of the smaller ethnicities to create another panel view

Code 
states_shp3 <- states_shp %>% 
  right_join(migrate_race, by = c("STATE_NAME" = "PREV_STATENAME"))

states_shp3 <- states_shp3 %>%
  filter(RACE_ETHNIC != 'American Indian',
         RACE_ETHNIC != 'Mixed',
         RACE_ETHNIC != 'Other')



ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = states_shp3, aes(fill=interstate_mig_rate_state_race), size=0.1) +
  coord_sf(crs = "epsg:5070") +
  scale_fill_distiller(name=' ', palette='BuPu',direction=1,
                      breaks=c(.0, .02, .04, .06, .08, .1, 0.12, 0.14, 0.16),
                      labels=label_percent(accuracy = 1),
                      limits=c(0,.16)) +
  labs(title = "Racial Migration rates, 2022-2023") +
  theme(panel.grid.major = element_blank(), 
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        panel.background = element_blank(), 
        axis.line = element_blank(), 
        axis.text.x=element_blank(),
        axis.text.y=element_blank(),
        axis.ticks=element_blank(),
        axis.title=element_blank(),
        legend.key.size =  unit(0.25, "in"),
        legend.text = element_text(size=8),
        legend.title = element_text(size=6),
        plot.title = element_text(size=14,hjust = 0.5),
        plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5),        
        plot.margin=unit(c(1, 1, 1, 1), "points"),
        strip.background = element_rect(fill='white'),
        strip.text.x = element_text(size = 10)) +
  facet_wrap(~RACE_ETHNIC)

If it was up to me a lot of the states that have higher percentages here have low populations in the respective states. Idaho and Montana are full of White people hence why there are not as many Black or Asian people, but Latino communities do exist in these states. North Dakota does not have many Latinos. Mississippi also does not have many Asians. I think to get more of an introspective view on this would be to adjust the percentages to observe significant discrepencies between race.

4.9 Calculate and make a dataframe for our age cohorts

Code 
migrate_age_cohorts <- data %>% 
  filter(AGE>=1) %>% 
  mutate(agegroup=cut(AGE, breaks=c(0,18,31,65,500), right=F,
                      labels=c("1-17","18-30","31-64", "65+"))) %>% 
  group_by(PREV_STATENAME, agegroup) %>% 
  summarise(pop=sum(PERWT),
         interstate_movers=sum(PERWT[MIGRATE1==3])) %>%
  mutate(interstate_mig_rate_age=interstate_movers/pop) %>% 
  ungroup()
`summarise()` has grouped output by 'PREV_STATENAME'. You can override using
the `.groups` argument.
Code 
gt(head(migrate_age_cohorts))
State (FIPS code) agegroup pop interstate_movers interstate_mig_rate_age
Alabama 1-17 1066624 14198 0.013311
Alabama 18-30 846126 41755 0.049348
Alabama 31-64 2179988 35132 0.016116
Alabama 65+ 932721 10785 0.011563
Alaska 1-17 161609 7166 0.044342
Alaska 18-30 130666 13043 0.099819

4.10 Perform a right join to keep everything in the migrate_age_cohorts and plot our grid again of our 4 selected age cohorts

Code 
states_shp4 <- states_shp %>% 
  right_join(migrate_age_cohorts, by = c("STATE_NAME" = "PREV_STATENAME"))

ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = states_shp4, aes(fill=interstate_mig_rate_age), size=0.1) +
  coord_sf(crs = "epsg:5070") +
  scale_fill_distiller(name=' ', palette='BuPu',direction=1,
                      breaks=c(.0, .01, .02, .03, .04, .05, .06, .07, .08),
                      labels=label_percent(accuracy = 1),
                      limits=c(0,.08)) +
  labs(title = "Age Group Migration rates, 2022-2023") +
  theme(panel.grid.major = element_blank(), 
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        panel.background = element_blank(), 
        axis.line = element_blank(), 
        axis.text.x=element_blank(),
        axis.text.y=element_blank(),
        axis.ticks=element_blank(),
        axis.title=element_blank(),
        legend.key.size =  unit(0.25, "in"),
        legend.text = element_text(size=8),
        legend.title = element_text(size=6),
        plot.title = element_text(size=14,hjust = 0.5),
        plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5),        
        plot.margin=unit(c(1, 1, 1, 1), "points"),
        strip.background = element_rect(fill='white'),
        strip.text.x = element_text(size = 10)) +
  facet_wrap(~agegroup)

4.11 Calculate location quotient compared to total rates by State and graph that according to each age group

Code 
states_shp4 <- states_shp4 %>%
  mutate(
    lq_state_mig_rate_age=interstate_mig_rate_age/interstate_mig_rate_origin)


ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = states_shp4, aes(fill=lq_state_mig_rate_age), size=0.1) +
  coord_sf(crs = "epsg:5070") +
  scale_fill_distiller(name=' ', palette='BuPu',direction=1,
                      breaks=c(.0, .5, 1, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 3.5),
                      limits=c(0,3.5)) +
  labs(title = "LQ of Age Group Migration Rates by Total State Migration Rate, 2022-2023") +
  theme(panel.grid.major = element_blank(), 
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        panel.background = element_blank(), 
        axis.line = element_blank(), 
        axis.text.x=element_blank(),
        axis.text.y=element_blank(),
        axis.ticks=element_blank(),
        axis.title=element_blank(),
        legend.key.size =  unit(0.25, "in"),
        legend.text = element_text(size=8),
        legend.title = element_text(size=6),
        plot.title = element_text(size=14,hjust = 0.5),
        plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5),        
        plot.margin=unit(c(1, 1, 1, 1), "points"),
        strip.background = element_rect(fill='white'),
        strip.text.x = element_text(size = 10)) +
  facet_wrap(~agegroup)

Unsurprisingly the age compromising of most college grads and young people contains significantly higher rates of moving compared rest of their respective states. Although it may be hard to tell, old people are moving a decent bit less whereas the 31-64 cohort seems to be relatively consistent with the overall state migration rate

4.12 Calculate national rate of migration for age cohorts and compare that by state. I used the none shapefile because there was weird issues here with the geometry messing with the variable being found.

Code 
national_cohort_mig_rate <- migrate_age_cohorts %>%
  # st_drop_geometry()
  group_by(agegroup) %>%
  summarise(
    pop_age=sum(pop),
    interstate_mover_age=sum(interstate_movers)) %>%
  mutate(national_age_mig_rate=interstate_mover_age/pop_age) %>%
  ungroup()

gt(national_cohort_mig_rate)
agegroup pop_age interstate_mover_age national_age_mig_rate
1-17 69091574 1180457 0.017085
18-30 57034535 2993519 0.052486
31-64 145967692 2765323 0.018945
65+ 59329416 683321 0.011517

4.13 Now we will go ahead and merge this issue to our shapefile, compute the location quotient of national migration rates of the age cohorts compared to the state age cohort migration rate

Code 
## left_join since we need to keep everything in states_shp4
states_shp4 <- states_shp4 %>%
  right_join(national_cohort_mig_rate, by = "agegroup")

## Calculate location quotient over national migrate rate for age cohort
states_shp4 <- states_shp4 %>%
  mutate(
    lq_mig_rate_cohort=interstate_mig_rate_age/national_age_mig_rate
  )

ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = states_shp4, aes(fill=lq_mig_rate_cohort), size=0.1) +
  coord_sf(crs = "epsg:5070") +
  scale_fill_distiller(name=' ', palette='BuPu',direction=1,
                      breaks=c(.0, .4, .8, 1.2, 1.6, 2, 2.4, 2.8),
                      limits=c(0, 3.02)) +
  labs(title = "LQ of Age Group Migration Rates by National Age Cohort Migration Rate, 2022-2023") +
  theme(panel.grid.major = element_blank(), 
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        panel.background = element_blank(), 
        axis.line = element_blank(), 
        axis.text.x=element_blank(),
        axis.text.y=element_blank(),
        axis.ticks=element_blank(),
        axis.title=element_blank(),
        legend.key.size =  unit(0.25, "in"),
        legend.text = element_text(size=8),
        legend.title = element_text(size=6),
        plot.title = element_text(size=14,hjust = 0.5),
        plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5),        
        plot.margin=unit(c(1, 1, 1, 1), "points"),
        strip.background = element_rect(fill='white'),
        strip.text.x = element_text(size = 10)) +
  facet_wrap(~agegroup)

When we compare to the overall age cohorts we can see Alaska is quite a popular state to leave as well as Wyoming both higher in most cohorts. I find it interesting how New Mexico is higher amongst the youngest group but remains only slightly high for other groups. Of course, this location quotient does undermine the overall veracity of younger people migrating but there is not a clear state ahead of the rest except for perhaps wyoming and both Vermont and New Hampshire. I will note that D.C. had quite high for most groups although that cannot be seen well from these maps.
